site.name: PromptCraft Academy
site.tagline: A GitHub-ready, zero-cost multi-page GenAI learning experience
site.description: This site consolidates three beginner workbooks into an approachable, interactive website that teaches AI, machine learning, and generative AI through guided tracks, chapter notes, revision pages, teacher-led perspectives, and hands-on practice.

highlights: 100 bite-sized workbook lessons grouped into 20 topic tracks
5 detailed beginner chapters with practice exercises
15 quick-revision topic summaries for fast review
5 teacher perspectives to make the content feel more dynamic
Static hosting friendly architecture for GitHub Pages

tracks.title: AI Foundations
tracks.summary: AI is the umbrella term for computer systems that perform tasks normally associated with human intelligence, such as recognizing patterns, understanding language, making decisions, and adapting from experience.

tracks.title: Machine Learning Basics
tracks.summary: Machine learning enables software to improve its performance on a task by learning patterns from data rather than relying only on fixed rules written by humans.

tracks.title: Core ML Algorithms
tracks.summary: Linear regression predicts a continuous number by fitting a line or plane that minimizes prediction error. It is a beginner-friendly way to understand loss, training, and prediction.

tracks.title: Deep Learning and Neural Networks
tracks.summary: A neural network is a layered mathematical system that transforms inputs into outputs. During training, it adjusts internal weights to reduce errors.

tracks.title: Generative AI Fundamentals
tracks.summary: Generative AI creates new content based on patterns learned from training data. It can produce text, code, images, audio, video, synthetic data, and design variations.

tracks.title: Large Language Models
tracks.summary: A large language model processes tokens and predicts likely next tokens. By repeating this process, it can generate sentences, explanations, code, summaries, and conversations.

tracks.title: Training and Alignment
tracks.summary: Pre-training teaches a model broad patterns from huge datasets. For language models, this often involves predicting hidden or next tokens from large text collections.

tracks.title: Prompt Engineering
tracks.summary: Prompt engineering is the practice of writing clear instructions, context, examples, and constraints so an AI model produces a useful output.

tracks.title: RAG and Context Engineering
tracks.summary: Retrieval-augmented generation connects an LLM to external knowledge. The system retrieves relevant information and adds it to the prompt before the model answers.

tracks.title: AI Agents and Agentic AI
tracks.summary: AI agents are software systems that use AI to pursue goals and complete tasks on behalf of users. They may reason, plan, use tools, remember state, and adapt.

tracks.title: Multimodal AI
tracks.summary: Multimodal AI can understand or generate more than one data type, such as text, images, audio, video, tables, charts, and documents.

tracks.title: Responsible AI and Safety
tracks.summary: Responsible AI is the practice of designing, building, and using AI systems in ways that are fair, reliable, safe, private, secure, transparent, and accountable.

tracks.title: Building AI Applications
tracks.summary: Before building AI, define the user problem, success metric, available data, risks, constraints, and whether AI is actually needed.

tracks.title: MLOps and LLMOps
tracks.summary: MLOps applies software engineering, DevOps, and data practices to machine learning so models can be built, deployed, monitored, and improved reliably.

tracks.title: AI Tools and Ecosystem
tracks.summary: AI assistants help users draft, summarize, explain, brainstorm, code, translate, and analyze information through natural language interaction.

tracks.title: Business Applications
tracks.summary: AI can route tickets, suggest responses, summarize conversations, detect sentiment, and power chatbots that answer common questions.

tracks.title: Career and Skills
tracks.summary: Python is widely used in AI because of its readable syntax and strong libraries for data, machine learning, deep learning, automation, and application development.

tracks.title: Hands-on Practice
tracks.summary: A beginner classifier project teaches data collection, labeling, preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and deployment thinking.

tracks.title: Advanced GenAI Concepts
tracks.summary: Temperature, top-p, and related sampling settings influence how predictable or creative model outputs are. Lower settings tend to be more focused, while higher settings may add variety but also more risk of errors.

tracks.title: Data and Evaluation
tracks.summary: AI systems are limited by the quality of their data. Clean, representative, well-labeled, and legally usable data improves model reliability.

chapters.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?

sections.heading: Understanding AI
sections.summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the ability of machines to mimic human intelligence. This means computers can learn, reason, make decisions, recognize patterns, and solve problems. AI is everywhere around us — from YouTube recommendations to voice assistants like Alexa and Siri.
sections.beginnerTip: Think about this concept in a real-life scenario. Try explaining it to a friend using simple language. If you can explain it simply, you truly understand it.

sections.heading: Real-World Examples
sections.summary: Examples of AI include Netflix recommendations, Google Maps route suggestions, face recognition in smartphones, self-driving cars, chatbots, spam email filtering, and fraud detection systems in banks.
sections.beginnerTip: Think about this concept in a real-life scenario. Try explaining it to a friend using simple language. If you can explain it simply, you truly understand it.

sections.heading: How AI Works
sections.summary: AI systems work by collecting data, finding patterns in that data, and making predictions. The more data the AI sees, the better it becomes at understanding and predicting outcomes.
sections.beginnerTip: Think about this concept in a real-life scenario. Try explaining it to a friend using simple language. If you can explain it simply, you truly understand it.

sections.heading: Types of AI
sections.summary: Weak AI performs specific tasks such as voice assistants. Strong AI refers to human-level intelligence, which still does not exist today.
sections.beginnerTip: Think about this concept in a real-life scenario. Try explaining it to a friend using simple language. If you can explain it simply, you truly understand it.

chapters.title: Introduction to Machine Learning

sections.heading: What is Machine Learning?
sections.summary: Machine Learning (ML) is a branch of AI where systems learn from data instead of being explicitly programmed. Instead of writing every rule manually, we allow computers to identify patterns on their own.

sections.heading: Supervised Learning
sections.summary: In supervised learning, models learn from labeled data. For example, if we show thousands of pictures labeled as cats or dogs, the system learns to identify them automatically.

revisionTopics.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
points: Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to systems that can perform tasks requiring human intelligence such as reasoning, learning, problem-solving, and decision-making.
AI systems can analyze large amounts of data, identify patterns, and improve their performance over time.
Examples include Siri, Alexa, recommendation systems, self-driving cars, and chatbots.
Weak AI focuses on specific tasks, while Strong AI aims to achieve human-like intelligence.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: History and Evolution of AI
points: 1950: Alan Turing introduced the Turing Test to determine whether machines can mimic human intelligence.
1956: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was officially coined by John McCarthy.
1997: IBM Deep Blue defeated chess champion Garry Kasparov.
Modern AI growth accelerated due to big data, cloud computing, GPUs, and advanced algorithms.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Machine Learning Fundamentals
points: Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI where machines learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed.
Supervised Learning uses labeled data.
Unsupervised Learning finds hidden patterns in unlabeled data.
Reinforcement Learning trains systems using rewards and penalties.
Applications include fraud detection, recommendation systems, and predictive analytics.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Deep Learning and Neural Networks
points: Deep Learning uses neural networks with multiple layers to process complex information.
Neural networks imitate how the human brain processes information.
Deep learning powers image recognition, speech recognition, and large language models.
Popular frameworks include TensorFlow and PyTorch.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Generative AI Fundamentals
points: Generative AI creates new content such as text, images, code, music, and videos.
Unlike traditional AI, it generates original outputs instead of only analyzing data.
Examples include ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Midjourney, and DALL·E.
Generative AI models are trained on massive datasets to learn patterns and generate realistic outputs.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Large Language Models (LLMs)
points: Large Language Models are neural networks trained to predict the next word or token in a sequence.
LLMs understand language patterns and context using billions of parameters.
Examples include GPT models, Gemini, Llama, and Claude.
LLMs use transformer architecture and attention mechanisms to process language.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Tokenization, Vectors, and Embeddings
points: Tokenization breaks text into smaller pieces called tokens.
Tokens may represent words, parts of words, or symbols.
Embeddings convert words into vectors (numerical representations).
Similar words are placed close together in vector space.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Transformers and Attention Mechanism
points: Transformers revolutionized AI by improving contextual understanding.
Attention mechanisms help models understand the meaning of words based on surrounding context.
For example, the word 'Apple' can mean a fruit or a company depending on nearby words.
Transformers made modern chatbots and generative AI possible.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: Prompt Engineering
points: Prompt engineering is the process of designing instructions for AI systems.
Clear and structured prompts improve AI outputs.
Common techniques include role prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and few-shot prompting.
Effective prompts help generate better code, summaries, stories, and analysis.
revisionTopics.beginnerTip: Focus on understanding the concepts conceptually before moving into coding or advanced implementation.

revisionTopics.title: AI Agents and Agentic AI
points: AI agents combine LLMs with tools and memory to perform actions.
Examples include coding assistants and autonomous workflows.
Agentic AI can coordinate multiple tasks, make decisions, and adapt dynamically.
AI agents can use APIs, databases, and external tools to complete tasks.

revisionTopics.title: Multimodal AI
points: Multimodal AI can process and generate multiple types of data such as text, images, audio, and video.
Examples include image generation tools and voice assistants.
Modern AI systems combine different data formats for richer understanding.
Applications include healthcare, entertainment, robotics, and content creation.

Cloud platforms provide scalable AI infrastructure.

teacherLessons.summary: AI is the umbrella term for computer systems that perform tasks normally associated with human intelligence, such as recognizing patterns, understanding language, making decisions, and adapting from experience.

teacherLessons.moduleTitle: Weak AI, Strong AI, and Superintelligence
teacherLessons.summary: Weak or narrow AI solves specific problems, while strong AI or AGI refers to a hypothetical system that could reason broadly like a human. Artificial superintelligence refers to a still-hypothetical system that would exceed human capability in most areas.
AI concepts are old, but modern adoption increased because of more data, better algorithms, faster hardware, cloud infrastructure, open-source tools, and business demand for automation.

teacherLessons.moduleTitle: AI, ML, Deep Learning, and Generative AI
teacherLessons.summary: AI is the broad field. Machine learning is a subset where systems learn from data. Deep learning is a subset of ML based on neural networks. Generative AI is a family of models that create new text, images, audio, video, or code.

teacherLessons.moduleTitle: AI Use Cases in Daily Life
teacherLessons.summary: AI appears in search engines, recommendations, maps, fraud detection, translation, spam filters, image search, customer support bots, and productivity tools.
Machine learning enables software to improve its performance on a task by learning patterns from data rather than relying only on fixed rules written by humans.
Supervised learning trains models using examples where the correct answer is known. The model studies input-output pairs and learns to predict the output for new inputs.
Unsupervised learning finds structure in data without labeled answers. It is useful for grouping, discovering patterns, reducing dimensions, and understanding data distributions.
Reinforcement learning trains an agent to act in an environment by receiving rewards or penalties. It is useful when learning happens through trial, feedback, and long-term outcomes.
A dataset is often split into training, validation, and test sets. Training fits the model, validation helps tune choices, and testing estimates real-world performance.
Linear regression predicts a continuous number by fitting a line or plane that minimizes prediction error. It is a beginner-friendly way to understand loss, training, and prediction.
Logistic regression is used for classification, especially binary classification. It estimates the probability that an example belongs to a class.
A decision tree makes predictions by asking a sequence of questions. Each branch narrows down the answer until the model reaches a prediction.
An ensemble combines many models to get a stronger result. A random forest combines many decision trees and averages or votes across them.
Evaluation metrics depend on the task. Accuracy is not enough when classes are imbalanced. Precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, MAE, and RMSE help measure different aspects of performance.
A neural network is a layered mathematical system that transforms inputs into outputs. During training, it adjusts internal weights to reduce errors.

teacherLessons.moduleTitle: Why Deep Learning Needs Data and Compute
teacherLessons.summary: Deep learning models can learn complex patterns, but they usually need large datasets and powerful hardware because they perform many matrix operations during training.
Activation functions allow neural networks to learn non-linear relationships. Without them, even a deep network would behave like a simple linear model.
Training uses loss to measure error. Backpropagation calculates how each weight contributed to the error, and gradient descent updates weights to reduce future error.
Different neural architectures are suited to different data. CNNs are strong for images, RNNs were common for sequences, and transformers became dominant for language and many multimodal tasks.
Generative AI creates new content based on patterns learned from training data. It can produce text, code, images, audio, video, synthetic data, and design variations.
Traditional AI often predicts, classifies, recommends, or detects. Generative AI creates outputs that feel new, such as paragraphs, images, music, and code.
A foundation model is a general-purpose model trained on large amounts of data and adaptable to many downstream tasks through prompting, fine-tuning, or retrieval.
A modality is a type of data, such as text, image, audio, video, 3D, or structured data. Modern systems increasingly combine multiple modalities.
Generative AI may hallucinate, repeat bias, misunderstand context, leak sensitive information if poorly designed, or produce unsafe content without guardrails.
A large language model processes tokens and predicts likely next tokens. By repeating this process, it can generate sentences, explanations, code, summaries, and conversations.
Text is split into tokens before entering a model. The context window is the amount of tokens the model can consider at once.
Embeddings turn tokens, sentences, or documents into vectors. Similar meanings appear closer in vector space, enabling semantic search and retrieval.
Attention helps a model decide which tokens matter for understanding another token. It allows the model to use context, such as knowing whether Apple means fruit or company.
Autoregressive models generate one token at a time. Each new token becomes part of the context for predicting the next token.
Pre-training teaches a model broad patterns from huge datasets. For language models, this often involves predicting hidden or next tokens from large text collections.
Self-supervised learning creates training signals from the data itself. For example, a model can learn by predicting missing words or next words without humans labeling every example.
Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model to a specific style, domain, or task by continuing training on curated examples.
Supervised fine-tuning uses example instructions and desired answers to teach a model how to behave like an assistant instead of merely continuing text.
Reinforcement learning from human feedback and preference optimization teach models to produce outputs that humans prefer, such as helpful, harmless, and concise answers.
Prompt engineering is the practice of writing clear instructions, context, examples, and constraints so an AI model produces a useful output.
A strong prompt can include role, task, context, input data, output format, constraints, tone, examples, and evaluation criteria.
Zero-shot prompting asks the model to solve a task without examples. Few-shot prompting gives sample inputs and outputs so the model can imitate the pattern.
Structured prompts request output in a specific format, such as JSON, a table, bullet points, lesson plan, or checklist.
Prompt engineering is experimental. You test prompts against real examples, compare outputs, revise wording, and document what works.
Retrieval-augmented generation connects an LLM to external knowledge. The system retrieves relevant information and adds it to the prompt before the model answers.
Vector databases store embeddings and allow similarity search. They help find documents that are semantically related to a user query.
Chunking divides documents into meaningful pieces. Good chunks are large enough to preserve meaning but small enough to retrieve precisely.
Prompt engineering focuses on one instruction. Context engineering designs how memory, retrieved documents, user preferences, tools, and summaries are assembled for each request.
RAG evaluation checks whether retrieval found the right evidence and whether the generated answer used it faithfully.
AI agents are software systems that use AI to pursue goals and complete tasks on behalf of users. They may reason, plan, use tools, remember state, and adapt.
Agents become useful when connected to tools such as search, calculators, databases, email, calendars, code execution, or business systems.
Agentic systems break a goal into smaller steps, execute them, observe results, and adjust the plan when needed.
Agent memory can include short-term conversation state, long-term preferences, task state, and external records.
More autonomy creates more risk. Agents can take wrong actions, use tools incorrectly, leak data, or overstep user intent.
Multimodal AI can understand or generate more than one data type, such as text, images, audio, video, tables, charts, and documents.
Image generation models create visuals from text prompts or edit existing images. They are used in design, marketing, storyboarding, and creative exploration.
Audio AI can transcribe speech, synthesize voices, remove noise, classify sounds, or generate music.
Video and 3D generation are emerging areas where models produce motion, scenes, characters, or 3D assets from prompts or references.
Multimodal RAG retrieves text, tables, charts, diagrams, or images so an AI system can answer questions grounded in mixed-format information.
Responsible AI is the practice of designing, building, and using AI systems in ways that are fair, reliable, safe, private, secure, transparent, and accountable.
Bias appears when data, design, or deployment causes unfair outcomes for groups or individuals. Fairness requires careful data review, testing, and monitoring.
AI systems must protect sensitive data. Risks include data leakage, prompt injection, model inversion, unauthorized tool use, and unsafe retention.
Hallucination occurs when a model produces incorrect information confidently. Grounding uses evidence, retrieval, tools, and validation to reduce this risk.
AI governance defines policies, roles, reviews, testing, documentation, monitoring, and escalation paths for AI systems.
Before building AI, define the user problem, success metric, available data, risks, constraints, and whether AI is actually needed.
Data preparation includes collecting, cleaning, labeling, formatting, splitting, and documenting data. It often takes more time than model selection.
Teams can build custom models, fine-tune existing models, use APIs, or combine off-the-shelf tools with custom data.
AI applications should be tested for accuracy, latency, cost, safety, usability, and failure modes before reaching users.
Deployment means making the AI system available to users. Monitoring checks quality, costs, errors, drift, abuse, and user satisfaction over time.
MLOps applies software engineering, DevOps, and data practices to machine learning so models can be built, deployed, monitored, and improved reliably.
Experiment tracking records datasets, code, parameters, metrics, and model versions so teams can reproduce results and compare approaches.
Model versioning helps teams know which model is in development, testing, production, or rollback.
LLMOps extends MLOps with prompts, retrieval indexes, safety filters, evaluation sets, model providers, token costs, and response monitoring.
AI systems must balance quality, speed, and cost. Smaller models, caching, batching, retrieval pruning, and prompt compression can help.
AI assistants help users draft, summarize, explain, brainstorm, code, translate, and analyze information through natural language interaction.
Coding assistants generate code, explain errors, write tests, refactor functions, and help navigate unfamiliar codebases.
Open-source model ecosystems allow learners and teams to explore models, datasets, demos, and tooling without depending only on closed platforms.
Cloud AI platforms provide infrastructure, managed models, deployment tools, monitoring, security, and integrations for building AI applications at scale.
No-code and low-code tools help non-developers create AI workflows, chatbots, dashboards, and automations with limited coding.
AI can route tickets, suggest responses, summarize conversations, detect sentiment, and power chatbots that answer common questions.
AI helps create campaign drafts, segment audiences, personalize content, score leads, summarize calls, and analyze customer feedback.
AI supports imaging analysis, documentation, triage assistance, drug discovery, patient communication, and research workflows, but requires strict validation.
AI supports fraud detection, risk scoring, financial analysis, forecasting, compliance review, and report generation.
AI can personalize learning, generate practice questions, explain concepts, summarize materials, and support teachers with planning.
Python is widely used in AI because of its readable syntax and strong libraries for data, machine learning, deep learning, automation, and application development.
Useful AI math includes linear algebra, probability, statistics, calculus basics, and optimization. Beginners can learn these gradually through examples.
A portfolio shows that you can apply concepts. Good beginner projects are small, practical, documented, and easy for others to run.
AI interviews often test fundamentals, project experience, trade-offs, evaluation, responsible AI, and practical problem-solving.
A strong learning path moves from AI basics to ML, deep learning, LLMs, prompt engineering, RAG, agents, MLOps, responsible AI, and projects.
A beginner classifier project teaches data collection, labeling, preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and deployment thinking.
A prompt library stores reusable prompts for summarization, tutoring, coding, data analysis, writing, and brainstorming.
A RAG bot lets users ask questions over documents. It requires chunking, embeddings, retrieval, prompting, citations, and answer evaluation.
A safe beginner agent can use tools such as calculator, search over local files, or task checklist generation without performing irreversible actions.
A capstone combines problem framing, data, AI model or API, evaluation, user interface, documentation, and responsible AI checks.

Temperature, top-p, and related sampling settings influence how predictable or creative model outputs are. Lower settings tend to be more focused, while higher settings may add variety but also more risk of errors.
A context window limits how much information the model can consider at one time. Long documents may need chunking, summarization, retrieval, or selective context assembly.
System and developer instructions define behavior, constraints, tone, and tool usage. They are usually more authoritative than ordinary user text in an application.
Prompt injection happens when user-provided or retrieved text tries to override the application instructions or misuse tools.
Choosing a model involves trade-offs between quality, cost, latency, context length, modality support, privacy, and tool integration.
AI systems are limited by the quality of their data. Clean, representative, well-labeled, and legally usable data improves model reliability.
Overfitting happens when a model memorizes training data too closely. Underfitting happens when the model is too simple to learn the pattern.
A confusion matrix shows true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives. It helps beginners understand classification errors.
Human evaluation is useful for generative AI because outputs can be subjective, open-ended, or hard to judge with one automatic metric.
Benchmarks are standardized tests, but real application quality depends on your own data, users, tasks, and constraints.
